title: Champagne Perrier Jouët have implemented regenerative viticulture in half of their vineyard area, and they are already seeing differences in the base wines
summary: Jamie Goode travels to Champagne to visit the vineyards of Perrier-Jouët. Five years ago they began trialling regenerative approaches and
content:
Jamie Goode travels to Champagne to visit the vineyards of Perrier-Jouët. Five years ago they began trialling regenerative approaches and now 33 of their 65 hectares of vines are farmed this way, with a view to becoming 100% regenerative by 2030. Significantly, they are already finding that their base wines taste different.

It’s mid-April, and I’m standing in one of the vineyard plots of Champagne Perrier-Jouët, in the Grand Cru village of Ambonnay. The buds on the vine are just bursting: these vineyards have dodged the spring frost that has afflicted some parts of the region this year, but there’s something very different about this plot, and the ones around it. While the rest of the surrounding vineyard area is bare and devoid of life, these are bursting with life and energy. The multispecies cover crops, sown in autumn, have been flourishing, and in particular the yellow of the mustard flower and the white flowers of the broad beans really catch the eye.

It was back in 2021 that they began with their regenerative farming project. The first year, 4 hectares of their 65 hectares of vines were farmed this way, and because of the ongoing success of these trials, they now have 33 hectares of their 65 hectares of vines farmed regeneratively.

Importantly, the trials were established in such a way that they could measure everything: it’s a scientific trial. So the vineyards enrolled in the program are separated into repeated blocks of three different treatments.

The first is the control, the témoin. This is the way that they have farmed up till now, and it’s certainly not a bad way of working. They haven’t used herbicides since 2000, so undervine weed control is done manually, by ploughing. Then the row itself is left grassed. This is a higher standard of viticulture than most of the region practices: in the Champagne vineyard of 33 000 hectares there are more than 16 000 growers, so many have small plots and this is not their main career. Herbicides are still widely used, because they are cheap and convenient.

Then we have two regenerative treatments. The structure of the vineyards in Champagne, with a high planting density of 10 000 vines per hectare, means that it’s hard to implement a full suite of regenerative practices because of the lack of space and the impact on an already close-planted vineyard of any extra competition. So their main approach has been to use multi-species cover cropping, with two different mixes.

The first is a biomass cover crop. This consists of fava bean (Vicia faba), rye, clover and alfalfa, which is sowed in September. This is then flattened in late April using a roller crimper, or is incorporated into the soil using a light discing. The roller crimper breaks the stems and terminates the growth, which then lies on the soil and acts as a mulch. This protects the surface of the soil, lowering soil temperatures and reducing water loss. In the increasingly warm growing seasons the region is experiencing, this is a benefit.

The idea behind the biomass crop is that as it decays it will release organic material into the soil. It also acts to fix atmospheric nitrogen. The crop has worked well, and Perrier-Jouët are seeing positive trends. Jessica Jazeron-Schneider, their vineyard transformation manager, points out some of the results. Compared with the control, the soil of both this and the second regenerative treatment (see below) has better porosity and structure. The cover can give up to 80 units of nitrogen, and they are seeing more pruning weight (an indicator of vigour), improved yields and also better quality grapes. They have also seen higher worm counts.

One of the concerns with having growth in the vineyard during the frost risk period is that cold air stays for longer, and they have seen this as a potential issue. But Ambonnay is not particularly frost-prone.

The second cover crop is the flower treatment. Here they have turnip, borage, fennel and mustard, as well as some alfalfa. The goal of this crop is to nourish the soil, as with biomass, but also to increase biodiversity. This crop is sown once every three years (the ones we are looking at are in their third year), and then it is flattened twice a year, once before vine treatment starts and then just before harvest. In the first mix alfalfa dominated everything, but they’ve now worked out the right ratios to sow.

They have two different sowing machines, because the floral cover requires the seeds to be just on the surface while the biomass crop is sown much more deeply. They will check the forecast before sowing in September an sow when rain is due shortly afterwards to help with germination.

The plan is to collect data for another three years, but already they have been impressed enough with the results that the plans are to include all their vineyards in the program by 2030.

Both of the cover crop treatments have seen an increase in yield of around 20%, while quality has improved.

And the most remarkable aspect about these trials is that already there’s a difference in the wines. With chef du cave Séverine Frerson I tasted through base wines from 2025 on two separate occasions, and was surprised by how different wines from the three treatments tasted. ‘At the beginning I wanted to analyse what this gave to the wine, and what the profile of each modality is,’ says Frerson.

We began with Chardonnay. These wines are from a plot in Oiri that has been divided into three: control, biomass cover and flower cover. All harvested same date, 25 August. Pressed in a 4 ton Willmes press, just using cuvée (the best bit of the press). The wines are fermented and aged in 2000 litre tanks.

Control (témoin)
Fruity, bright and lively with keen acidity. Rich citrus fruit with lots of fruit and some fine spicy hints.

Flower Cover
Juicy, bright and linear with lovely precision. Keen acidity supports bright lemony fruit, showing nice taut fruit. Leaner than control.

Biomass cover
Juicy and linear with nice keen citrus fruit and good acidity. Focused and purposeful. Tightest and leanest.

I asked Frerson: what differences do they find with their regenerative plots? ‘In terms of yield, flower cover gives the best yield,’ she says. ‘For nitrogen, the levels are higher with flower and biomass than the control. They wines have more colour intensity with biomass cover.’ Frerson says that flower cover gives more complexity and finesse in the wine, and the biomass cover gives more freshness – ‘it’s a very straight wine’. The profile of the regenerative wines are in line with the style of Perrier Jouët. ‘Biomass is very straight and vertical with a lot of freshness. The floral cover is more delicate: the texture of this wine is different.’ They have seen these differences since 2021. The control is never the best wine.

Then we tried Pinot Noir from Aÿ.

Control
Focused, fruity, lemony, a touch of cherry and green apple. Juicy and vivid, slightly compressed.

Flower cover
Much more expressive with bright lemon and mandarin fruit, as well as some cherry. Very bright and with lovely layers of flavour.

Biomass cover
Juicy and vivid with bright redcurrant, cherry and lemon fruit. Supple and with lovely acid line. Very fine.

To me, the two regenerative wines are much finer and more interesting.

They started tasting since 2021, and the first conclusion was that biomass is very interesting for Pinot Noir, and flower cover for Chardonnay is promising, but Severine thinks it is too early to decide. ‘Regenerative viticulture is very interesting for the style of Perrier Jouët,’ she says. ‘There are more floral notes, more fruitiness and more freshness.’ They want all their vineyards to be farmed regeneratively by 2030.

The real importance of this work is that it is proof of principle, showing other growers that this approach works. The hope is that others will adopt these regenerative practices and that the bare soils of Champagne in spring will be a thing of the past. Along these lines, several of partner growers are now showing an interest in regenerative viticulture, and they have a special team to work with them. They have received 25 growers into their vineyard to look at their regenerative program. The idea is to have a special contract for those who are going to farm regeneratively. They want to push these people towards this way of farming with knowledge support and higher prices for the grapes. The goal is to convince people it is a good choice for the soil and also the wine.

On my first visit in December 2025 I saw another element to their regenerative approach. This was a vitiforestry project, which they began in winter 2023. In a two hectare plot they have avoided the normal Champagne monoculture, and as well as planting vines they have given over 10% of the plot to biodiversity. They enlisted the help of naturalists and drew up a list of different species that would be suitable, and have planted in three strata: herbs, shrubs and trees. Of the 1000 shrubs and trees they planted, they got a 90% survival rate. It’s too soon to see the benefits, but the landscape of Champagne could really use a biodiversity injection like this, and as well as providing ecosystem services that may result in less spraying, this could make the vineyards more resilient in the face of climate change.

A film of the regenerative viticulture plots in Ambonnay:

[Disclosure: I was paid for my work with Perrier-Jouët, which included hosting a masterclass at Wine Paris, and helping with a press trip to explain the project to journalists in April 2026. ]

title: Restaurants: celebrating 10 years of the Barbary, Neal’s Yard, with its countertop-only dining spanning southern Europe to north Africa
summary: Website: https://www.thebarbary.co.uk/neals-yard Great meal last night at The Barbary, Neal’s Yard (London). It’s one of two locations for this amazing
content:
Website: https://www.thebarbary.co.uk/neals-yard

Great meal last night at The Barbary, Neal’s Yard (London). It’s one of two locations for this amazing restaurant, with the younger but larger sibling in Notting Hill.

Last night was a celebration of 10 years of The Barbary, and owners Layo and Zoë Paskin were present. Their story is a cool one: for many years they ran nightclubs and DJd, before turning to hospitality.

Back in 1995 Layo and Richard West (aka Mr C. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mr._C ) started nightclub ‘The End’.  Matthew Benjamin was the resident DJ, and from there Layo and Matthew started working and recording together as Layo and Bushwacka!. You can catch a flavour of this here:

https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCdq3eAKEU3OaFxuqvUrGR1Q

It was in 2014 that Layo and Zoë turned to restaurants, and they didn’t put a foot wrong. The current portfolio of Studio Paskin includes The Palomar, The Barbary (two locations), Michelin-starred Evelyn’s Table and upstairs wine bar The Mulwray.

The Barbary Neal’s Yard is a special, intimate place. The 24 covers are all arranged around a large, extended-U-shaped counter, with the kitchen in the middle. The centrepiece of the kitchen is a hybrid charcoal/gas grill, and there’s also a tandoor-style oven.

The service is assured and the food is fantastic. It’s all about flavour, but the experience of eating here transcends merely food and drink. There’s energy and vibe, as well as the visual spectacle of the kitchen in action.

The wine list is short and well chosen, offering a lot of variety, with most of the wines available by the glass. We drank the Druida Dão Branco from Mira d’O and a fleshy, perfumed Jura red (Domaine des Carlines Poulsard).

title: Highlights: Klein Constantia Vin de Constance 2023 South Africa
summary: This is the latest vintage of one of the world’s great sweet wines. It’s the Vin de Constance from Klein
content:
This is the latest vintage of one of the world’s great sweet wines. It’s the Vin de Constance from Klein Constantia. This was a wine that was globally famous, then production stopped, and then it was rebirthed. It’s a brilliant story.

In the 18th and 19th centuries, Constantia was one of the world’s great sweet wines, but it disappeared with the phylloxera crisis in the late 19th century. I got to try an 1875 Constantia once, which was a remarkable treat:

Constantia Sweet Wine 1875
Aromatics of leather, spices, old furniture. Raisins and table groups with some honey, too. Lovely aromatics: sweetly smelling and quite pristine. Honeyed and smooth on the palate with real harmony, and notes of barley sugar, grapes, stewed raisins, bread pudding and smooth honeyed harmony. There’s lovely weight here. Such a treat to drink this. 96/100 (it seems silly to rate this)

But since 1986 Klein Constantia have been making a sweet Constantia which they have labelled Vin de Constance based on the original wines that were made here. Back then, the original Constantia property was around 900 hectares. Part of the original vineyard is now suburbs, and the remainder is split into four properties: Klein Constantia, Buitenverwachting, Groot Constantia and Constantia Uitsig.

When the Jooste family bought the property in 1980, they were approached by a Stellenbosch University viticulturist, the late Professor Chris Orffer, who encouraged them to try to recreate the historical sweet wine. Orffer assisted the Klein Constantia team in their quest to produce something resembling the original as closely as possible, and through the skilled winemaking of the late Ross Gower, this was achieved.

Botrytis wasn’t a feature of the original Constantia wines, because it wasn’t present in the Cape at that time. For the sake of historical accuracy, therefore, Vin de Constance is a late-harvest style made from Muscat de Frontignan (aka Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains), without botrytis. First plantings of these Muscat vines were in 1982, and the first release of the new era Constantia was the 1986 vintage, released in 1990.

10% of the Muscat is picked early, to make a base wine at 12.5–13 % alcohol with good acidity. Then they go and pick raisined grapes. The remaining grapes are left to accumulate sugar, and leaf removal exposes the fruit to the sun, which helps raisin some of the remaining grapes. Then, the big pick takes place in three passages through the vineyard. Altogether, around 10% of the crop will be raisins. If the raisins have lots of sugar left in the skins, then the base wine is used to flush the flavour out. The skins are pressed quite hard, because tannins are an important part of the style. It’s not possible to settle this dirty juice, so it goes to barrels for fermentation.

Klein Constantia Vin de Constance 2023 Constantia, South Africa
14% alcohol. This has wonderful youthful, grapey, floral aromatics with a hint of raisin and spice. Initially it seems forward, sweet and easy, but there’s some spicy complexity lying under the vivid ester and terpenic prettiness. The palate is complex and intense with some spicy structure, as well as table grape, apricot and peach fruitiness. It’s sweet and rich, but serious, with lots of potential to develop in bottle. Currently the intense sweetness and primary fruity aromas are dominating, but there’s a lot to come here. This is a wine with an amazing track record. At the UK price (£60 for the 2022 at the Wine Society), I think this is a bargain if you are going to cellar it for a decade. You are drinking history. 95/100

title: Highlights: Champagne Bruno Paillard Blanc de Blancs 2015
summary: Champagne Bruno Paillard Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut 2015 France12% alcohol. From the warm, dry vintage of 2015 this is
content:
Champagne Bruno Paillard Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut 2015 France
12% alcohol. From the warm, dry vintage of 2015 this is 100% Chardonnay from the first pressings of grapes from Grand and Premier Cru vineyards in the Côtes des Blancs. It’s aged 7 years on lees, disgorged in June 2023 with a 3 g/l dosage. This is really harmonious, with some rich yellow fruit (plum, peach) as well as a touch of apple, and then some brioche and honey on the nose. The palate has some of this richness, but also some fresh crystalline citrus fruit and good acidity, together with a hint of structure. The solar vintage makes its mark: this has concentration and mineral extract, as well as some ripeness of fruit. But there’s no undue bitterness or harshness: everything works together really well. A beautifully complex Champagne. 95/100

title: Restaurants: Heard Burger, Soho, with some lovely Lambrusco Sorbara
summary: Website: https://heardburger.co.uk/ I’ve been to a few good Burger places of late. There’s the brilliant Gasoline Grill in Copenhagen, and
content:
Website: https://heardburger.co.uk/

I’ve been to a few good Burger places of late. There’s the brilliant Gasoline Grill in Copenhagen, and then I had the amazing cheeseburger at Fat Rabbit in St Catherines (Niagara, Canada) twice in a week. And while we were filming in Burgundy there was a brilliant roadside burger place in Beaune (Yellow Bus Burger). But Heard, a relatively new opening near Carnaby Street in London, is as good as I’ve tasted.

There’s limited space upstairs, so most of the action happens in the basement. As a space, it lacks any real character, but the burgers here are amazing. Thrilling even. And what got me excited was that they have a Laumbrusco Sorbara on the menu for £40. It’s a good one, too, from Cleto Chiarli. But for some bizarre reason, on the short but good wine list, this was in with the red wines. It’s a pale pink fizz, and anyone ordering this as a red will be quite surprised.

This is as good as burgers get, and a strong recommend from me, especially if you like wine with your burger (most people were drinking their burgers with soft drinks when we visited mid-afternoon, something I find hard to understand.)

Cleto Chiarli Vecchia Modena Lambrusco di Sorbara Premium 2024 Italy
11.5% alcohol. Comes sealed with an afgrafe (a cork with a metal clip). Pale pink in colour. Tart, bright and lively with a bit of bite as well as sweet cherry and cranberry fruit. So juicy and delicious with a tangy sherbetty finish, juicy and alive. 91/100

title: Highlights: Morandé La Capilla El Cabernet de Ránguil 2021 Itata, Chile
summary: This is quite special. It’s from 130 year old ungrafted vines planted on degraded granite soils, with a red clay
content:
This is quite special. It’s from 130 year old ungrafted vines planted on degraded granite soils, with a red clay matrix and a high presence of quartz. Aged 16 months in used French oak. It’s not normal to find old vine Cabernet in Itata: most of the old vines here are País, Cinsault or Muscat.

Morandé La Capilla El Cabernet de Ránguil 2021 Itata, Chile
13% alcohol. This is supple and elegant with delicious, fresh, slightly gravelly blackcurrant fruit with a chalky, mineral texture and nice focus and precision. There’s a faint hint of that Chilean pastille-like fruit character. It’s a refined, understated, elegant expression of this grape variety showing beautiful poise and balance. Supple and massively drinkable, but with some seriousness, too. I much prefer this style to the very ripe, full-on Chilean Cabernet Sauvignon style. 94/100

title: Highlights: Cecilia Wines Cinsault 2025 Piekenierskloof, South Africa
summary: Cinsault was historically a hugely important red grape for South Africa. Often, red ‘varietals’ from fancy grapes like Cabernet Sauvignon
content:
Cinsault was historically a hugely important red grape for South Africa. Often, red ‘varietals’ from fancy grapes like Cabernet Sauvignon would have a good dollop of Cinsault in them. It’s a variety that copes with heat really well, making fresh, lighter-style red wines. There’s less of it now than there used to be, but still quite a bit, and its time has come.

Cecilia Wines Cinsault 2025 Piekenierskloof, South Africa
13% alcohol. Cerina van Niekerk was previously the winemaker at Piekenierskloof. Supple and sappy with lovely red cherry and strawberry fruit. There’s a real focus to this wine, with some silkiness and sweetness to the fruit, with a touch of redcurrant, but also showing a bit of tannic crunch and good acidity. This is a beautiful expression of Cinsault, so nuanced and floral. 93/100 (£22 Stone Vine and Sun)

title: Excursions off the Riesling trail: what else can the Finger Lakes do?
summary: This was a seminar at the FLXcursion event that I’ve just come back from. The goal was to explore the
content:
This was a seminar at the FLXcursion event that I’ve just come back from. The goal was to explore the things that the Finger Lakes can do other than Riesling, its star grape variety.

The panel consisted of three: Tara Q Thomas, Jamal Rayyis and Lisa Granik. They pointed out that in 1900 there were 20 000 acres of grapes in the Finger Lakes region and they sold 7 million bottles of sparkling wine each year. The grape industry was thriving, and it was huge. Then prohibition came along, and after the war tastes changed. California was doing well, and the European sensibility in wine spread, and people changed their tastes away from the hybrid fare of New York State’s wine regions.

Then along came Konstantin Frank. At age 63 he started his own winery and he changed the view on whether or not it was possible to grow vinifera here. The industry is still reinventing itself, and there are still a lot of hybrids here. But we are now seeing a return to interesting hybrid grapes to make wines with personality.

These are the wines we tasted.

Konstantin Frank Rkatsiteli 2025 Finger Lakes, New York
A relatively neutral grape variety with thick skins and tending to low acidity. Light and open with nice citrus and white peach fruit as well as some citrus. Fruity and appealing with good texture and weight. Easy and expressive. 90/100

Casreli Marani Amidasturi 2022 Kakheti, Georgia
This is a blend of Kakhuri Mtsvane, Muskaturi Rkatsiteli and Rkatsiteli. Four months in qvevri on skins, no stems. Amber colour. Nice structure here with some tannins under the appealing, aromatic peach and pear fruit with a touch of baked apple. Has nice weight and structure. Really appealing, finishing firmly tannic and dry, but with such lovely open aromatics. 91/100

Standing Stone Saperavi 2024 Finger Lakes, New York
Deeply coloured, this has beautiful fresh black cherry and blackberry fruit with some nice grippy tannins and some acidity. Juicy but with some richness on the mid-palate. Chalky, grainy structure with a spicy bite on the finish. 93/100

Mukado Saperavi 2024 Kartli, Georgia
This is pure and nicely balanced with some tannic bite. Lovely berry and black fruits with some chalkiness and a bit of spicy detail on the finish. Really appealing. 93/100

Fox Run Grüner Veltliner 2025 Finger Lakes, New York
Nice weight here with pear and citrus fruit showing good texture, finishing fresh. Juicy and layered with a slight saltiness and a hint of white pepper. This is textural and appealing with nice layers of flavour. 92/100

Fritsch Ried Steinberg Grüner Veltliner 2023 Wagram, Austria
This is bright and textured with nice weight to the pear and citrus fruit with some herbal hints, some yellow fruit, and then some pepper notes on the finish. Nice weight to this wine. 92/100

Heart & Hands Esoterra 2023 Finger Lakes, New York
56% Auxerrois, 23% Aligoté, 21% Petite Arvine. This is supple, easy and fruity with sweet pear and table grape notes, a touch of creaminess and balanced acidity. Fruity and easy to drink. 89/100

Ambrositch Gemischter Satz Ringelspiel 2021 Wien, Austria
12 varieties. Fresh, fine, transparent and easy. Mandarin, lemon and pear fruit with a touch of watermelon. Light and expressive with real drinkability. 90/100

Keuka Lake Vineyards Skin-Fermented Vignoles 2021 Finger Lakes, New York
Two weeks on skins. Full yellow/gold. Good acidity and some tannic structure as well as apple and pear fruit. Nice weight here: it’s floral but there’s some spicy weight, finishing slightly sour. 90/100

Fio Wines Mosel Glou Glou 2024 Mosel, Germany
Pinot Blanc and Müller Thurgau. A month on skins. Cloudy with some nice mineral reductive character as well as fresh pear and citrus fruit. Juicy and expressive with freshness and nice texture. So drinkable and delicious. 93/100

Usonia Good Spirit 2024 Finger Lakes, New York
65% Chancellor, 20% Baco Noir, 10% Traminette, 5% Aromella. Some spicy bacon character here as well as juicy cherry and berry fruits. Fresh and supple with some pepper on the finish. Juicy and expressive. 90/100

Vignoble de Rêveur Rien ne Bouge 2023 Vin de France
60% Pinot Noir, 30% Pinot Gris, 10% Pinot Blanc. This is from Alsace. Lighter in colour, this is supple and smashable with red cherry and strawberry fruit. Such elegance and freshness with beautiful fruit. Fresh with nice acidity but also showing some silky red fruit character. Very fine. 94/100

Ravines Agricolae Keuka Red 2022 Finger Lakes, New York
Noiret and Cabernet Franc. Concentrated, textural and pepper with nice spicy detail and a bit of grip. Really distinctive and quite delicious. Grainy structure. 90/100

Marco Sara Schioppettino Friuli Colli Orientali 2023 Italy
Bright, peppery and vivid with some strawberry and red cherry. Textural and expressive, this is lighter in style but also has some mid-palate warmth. Textural and expressive. 92/100

title: Weingut Frank, one of the stars of Austria’s Weinviertel region
summary: Website: https://www.weingutfrank.at/englishhome Frank is a family winery that’s been going since 1724. They’re currently farming 23 hectares of vines, and
content:
Website: https://www.weingutfrank.at/englishhome

Frank is a family winery that’s been going since 1724. They’re currently farming 23 hectares of vines, and they are based in the far north east of the region.

They specialize in Grüner Veltliner, but also like to work with Riesling, which is 10% of their vineyard. ‘Grüner Veltliner is our most important variety, but we love Riesling,’ says Katrin Frank, who I tasted with at the 2026 edition of Vievinum. These are impressive wines.

Weingut Frank Grüner Veltliner Kalk Loess 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
11.5% alcohol. Fresh, linear and bright with a core of citrus fruit. Mineral and expressive with nice intensity and a hint of pepper. Stylish. 91/100

Weingut Frank Grüner Veltliner 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
12.5% alcohol. Linear, bright and fresh with nice tension, and some peppery notes. Good citrus drive with a crystalline quality to the fruit. Pure. 92/100

Weingut Frank Grüner Veltliner Ried Johannisberger 2024 Weinviertel, Austria
Single vineyard with loess soils. Stainless steel ageing on full lees. Textural with notes of white pepper, showing good focus and intensity. Peppery and fruity. 93/100

Weingut Frank Grüner Veltliner Ried Hoher Weg 2024 Weinviertel, Austria
13.5% alcohol. This is a smaller part of Johannisberger. It’s a windy spot, and soils are loess. 600 litre barrels. Textured, fine and delicate with some depth. White pepper, pear and citrus. Rounded, but finishes slightly salty. 94/100

Weingut Frank Gruner Veltliner Baumgartleiten 2023 Weinviertel, Austria
Limestone soils, raised in 600 litre barrels. Fresh, linear and textured. Bright with nice energy, and some notes of pepper as well as crystalline lemons. Lovely focus and refinement. 94/100

Weingut Frank Riesling Herrnbaumgarten 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
A village-level wine. Linear, bright and fruity with lovely texture. So expressive with nice fresh fruit but also good texture. Really good. 93/100

Weingut Frank Pinot Blanc Herrnbaumgarten 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
Linear, bright and fruity. Textural and expressive with nice fresh fruit but also some nice texture. 92/100

Weingut Frank Zweigelt 2024 Weinviertel, Austria
Lovely floral aromatics here. Really pure and vivid, and so inviting. Nice fruit, with a pure, joyful personalitu and good balance. 93/100

Weingut Frank Ried Krainholz Pinot Noir 2023 Wienviertel, Austria
The soils are a mix of loess and limestone. Supple and elegant with fine red cherry fruit as well as some strawberry, with fine-grained structure. Such a lovely wine. 94/100

title: The wines of Weingut Kurz, Weinviertel, Austria
summary: Website: https://www.weingutkurz.at/ Eric Kurz (pictured above) is the third generation in this family winery, and makes 80% white wines and 20%
content:
Website: https://www.weingutkurz.at/

Eric Kurz (pictured above) is the third generation in this family winery, and makes 80% white wines and 20% red, working alongside his parents Paul and Erika. Grüner Veltliner is the main variety here. They are based in the centre of the region. I tasted with Eric at VieVinum in May 2026.

Kurz Welschriesling 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
13% alcohol. Aromatic, with a floral, fresh fruity drive. Fresh, stony and bright on the palate, showing nice weight to the dry fruit. Driven and expressive. 90/100

Kurz G’Mischta 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
12% alcohol. A blend of Grüner Veltliner and Riesling with a dribble of Sauvignon. Aromatic, fruity and expressive with nice juicy citrus and a bit of grapiness. Bright and slightly pithy. 90/100

Kurz Ried Edelsbergen Grüner Veltliner 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
13% alcohol. Fresh and juicy with nice brightness to the citrus fruit. Light and very fruity, showing good precision and nice weight. Understated. 91/100

Kurz Ried Hainthal Grüner Veltliner 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
Juicy, supple and bright with nice energy, and a slight peppery edge to the layered citrus fruit. Pure with good acidity and some freshness. 91/100

Kurz Ried Hochberg Sauvignon Blanc 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
12.5% alcohol. Delicately herby. Subtle, stony and bright, with nice purity. Has a lightness to it. 88/100

Kurz Ried Kirchbergen Gelber Muskateller 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
13% alcohol. Bright and fruity with nice grippy detail and some citrus drive. Juicy and lively with nice herbal hints. 89/100

Kurz Grüner Veltliner Ried Hungherm Reserve 2022 Weinviertel, Austria
14% alcohol. Aged in new French oak for 18 months. Full yellow in colour, this is bold and rich with notes of melon, pear and peach. Nicely intense with good weight. Low-ish acidity, with notes of honey and toast in the mix. Bold style. 91/100

Kurz Roesler Ried Edelsbergen Weinviertel, Austria
14% alcohol. There are just 400 hectares of Roesler (a red grape) in Austria, and this is a blend of 2023 and 2024 aged in American oak. Deeply coloured, this is bold, rich, spicy and dense with vivid spicy notes alongside the sweet black fruits. Satisfyingly broad. 90/100

title: Champagne Perrier Jouët have implemented regenerative viticulture in half of their vineyard area, and they are already seeing differences in the base wines
summary: Jamie Goode travels to Champagne to visit the vineyards of Perrier-Jouët. Five years ago they began trialling regenerative approaches and
content:
Jamie Goode travels to Champagne to visit the vineyards of Perrier-Jouët. Five years ago they began trialling regenerative approaches and now 33 of their 65 hectares of vines are farmed this way, with a view to becoming 100% regenerative by 2030. Significantly, they are already finding that their base wines taste different.

It’s mid-April, and I’m standing in one of the vineyard plots of Champagne Perrier-Jouët, in the Grand Cru village of Ambonnay. The buds on the vine are just bursting: these vineyards have dodged the spring frost that has afflicted some parts of the region this year, but there’s something very different about this plot, and the ones around it. While the rest of the surrounding vineyard area is bare and devoid of life, these are bursting with life and energy. The multispecies cover crops, sown in autumn, have been flourishing, and in particular the yellow of the mustard flower and the white flowers of the broad beans really catch the eye.

It was back in 2021 that they began with their regenerative farming project. The first year, 4 hectares of their 65 hectares of vines were farmed this way, and because of the ongoing success of these trials, they now have 33 hectares of their 65 hectares of vines farmed regeneratively.

Importantly, the trials were established in such a way that they could measure everything: it’s a scientific trial. So the vineyards enrolled in the program are separated into repeated blocks of three different treatments.

The first is the control, the témoin. This is the way that they have farmed up till now, and it’s certainly not a bad way of working. They haven’t used herbicides since 2000, so undervine weed control is done manually, by ploughing. Then the row itself is left grassed. This is a higher standard of viticulture than most of the region practices: in the Champagne vineyard of 33 000 hectares there are more than 16 000 growers, so many have small plots and this is not their main career. Herbicides are still widely used, because they are cheap and convenient.

Then we have two regenerative treatments. The structure of the vineyards in Champagne, with a high planting density of 10 000 vines per hectare, means that it’s hard to implement a full suite of regenerative practices because of the lack of space and the impact on an already close-planted vineyard of any extra competition. So their main approach has been to use multi-species cover cropping, with two different mixes.

The first is a biomass cover crop. This consists of fava bean (Vicia faba), rye, clover and alfalfa, which is sowed in September. This is then flattened in late April using a roller crimper, or is incorporated into the soil using a light discing. The roller crimper breaks the stems and terminates the growth, which then lies on the soil and acts as a mulch. This protects the surface of the soil, lowering soil temperatures and reducing water loss. In the increasingly warm growing seasons the region is experiencing, this is a benefit.

The idea behind the biomass crop is that as it decays it will release organic material into the soil. It also acts to fix atmospheric nitrogen. The crop has worked well, and Perrier-Jouët are seeing positive trends. Jessica Jazeron-Schneider, their vineyard transformation manager, points out some of the results. Compared with the control, the soil of both this and the second regenerative treatment (see below) has better porosity and structure. The cover can give up to 80 units of nitrogen, and they are seeing more pruning weight (an indicator of vigour), improved yields and also better quality grapes. They have also seen higher worm counts.

One of the concerns with having growth in the vineyard during the frost risk period is that cold air stays for longer, and they have seen this as a potential issue. But Ambonnay is not particularly frost-prone.

The second cover crop is the flower treatment. Here they have turnip, borage, fennel and mustard, as well as some alfalfa. The goal of this crop is to nourish the soil, as with biomass, but also to increase biodiversity. This crop is sown once every three years (the ones we are looking at are in their third year), and then it is flattened twice a year, once before vine treatment starts and then just before harvest. In the first mix alfalfa dominated everything, but they’ve now worked out the right ratios to sow.

They have two different sowing machines, because the floral cover requires the seeds to be just on the surface while the biomass crop is sown much more deeply. They will check the forecast before sowing in September an sow when rain is due shortly afterwards to help with germination.

The plan is to collect data for another three years, but already they have been impressed enough with the results that the plans are to include all their vineyards in the program by 2030.

Both of the cover crop treatments have seen an increase in yield of around 20%, while quality has improved.

And the most remarkable aspect about these trials is that already there’s a difference in the wines. With chef du cave Séverine Frerson I tasted through base wines from 2025 on two separate occasions, and was surprised by how different wines from the three treatments tasted. ‘At the beginning I wanted to analyse what this gave to the wine, and what the profile of each modality is,’ says Frerson.

We began with Chardonnay. These wines are from a plot in Oiri that has been divided into three: control, biomass cover and flower cover. All harvested same date, 25 August. Pressed in a 4 ton Willmes press, just using cuvée (the best bit of the press). The wines are fermented and aged in 2000 litre tanks.

Control (témoin)
Fruity, bright and lively with keen acidity. Rich citrus fruit with lots of fruit and some fine spicy hints.

Flower Cover
Juicy, bright and linear with lovely precision. Keen acidity supports bright lemony fruit, showing nice taut fruit. Leaner than control.

Biomass cover
Juicy and linear with nice keen citrus fruit and good acidity. Focused and purposeful. Tightest and leanest.

I asked Frerson: what differences do they find with their regenerative plots? ‘In terms of yield, flower cover gives the best yield,’ she says. ‘For nitrogen, the levels are higher with flower and biomass than the control. They wines have more colour intensity with biomass cover.’ Frerson says that flower cover gives more complexity and finesse in the wine, and the biomass cover gives more freshness – ‘it’s a very straight wine’. The profile of the regenerative wines are in line with the style of Perrier Jouët. ‘Biomass is very straight and vertical with a lot of freshness. The floral cover is more delicate: the texture of this wine is different.’ They have seen these differences since 2021. The control is never the best wine.

Then we tried Pinot Noir from Aÿ.

Control
Focused, fruity, lemony, a touch of cherry and green apple. Juicy and vivid, slightly compressed.

Flower cover
Much more expressive with bright lemon and mandarin fruit, as well as some cherry. Very bright and with lovely layers of flavour.

Biomass cover
Juicy and vivid with bright redcurrant, cherry and lemon fruit. Supple and with lovely acid line. Very fine.

To me, the two regenerative wines are much finer and more interesting.

They started tasting since 2021, and the first conclusion was that biomass is very interesting for Pinot Noir, and flower cover for Chardonnay is promising, but Severine thinks it is too early to decide. ‘Regenerative viticulture is very interesting for the style of Perrier Jouët,’ she says. ‘There are more floral notes, more fruitiness and more freshness.’ They want all their vineyards to be farmed regeneratively by 2030.

The real importance of this work is that it is proof of principle, showing other growers that this approach works. The hope is that others will adopt these regenerative practices and that the bare soils of Champagne in spring will be a thing of the past. Along these lines, several of partner growers are now showing an interest in regenerative viticulture, and they have a special team to work with them. They have received 25 growers into their vineyard to look at their regenerative program. The idea is to have a special contract for those who are going to farm regeneratively. They want to push these people towards this way of farming with knowledge support and higher prices for the grapes. The goal is to convince people it is a good choice for the soil and also the wine.

On my first visit in December 2025 I saw another element to their regenerative approach. This was a vitiforestry project, which they began in winter 2023. In a two hectare plot they have avoided the normal Champagne monoculture, and as well as planting vines they have given over 10% of the plot to biodiversity. They enlisted the help of naturalists and drew up a list of different species that would be suitable, and have planted in three strata: herbs, shrubs and trees. Of the 1000 shrubs and trees they planted, they got a 90% survival rate. It’s too soon to see the benefits, but the landscape of Champagne could really use a biodiversity injection like this, and as well as providing ecosystem services that may result in less spraying, this could make the vineyards more resilient in the face of climate change.

A film of the regenerative viticulture plots in Ambonnay:

[Disclosure: I was paid for my work with Perrier-Jouët, which included hosting a masterclass at Wine Paris, and helping with a press trip to explain the project to journalists in April 2026. ]

title: Restaurants: celebrating 10 years of the Barbary, Neal’s Yard, with its countertop-only dining spanning southern Europe to north Africa
summary: Website: https://www.thebarbary.co.uk/neals-yard Great meal last night at The Barbary, Neal’s Yard (London). It’s one of two locations for this amazing
content:
Website: https://www.thebarbary.co.uk/neals-yard

Great meal last night at The Barbary, Neal’s Yard (London). It’s one of two locations for this amazing restaurant, with the younger but larger sibling in Notting Hill.

Last night was a celebration of 10 years of The Barbary, and owners Layo and Zoë Paskin were present. Their story is a cool one: for many years they ran nightclubs and DJd, before turning to hospitality.

Back in 1995 Layo and Richard West (aka Mr C. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mr._C ) started nightclub ‘The End’.  Matthew Benjamin was the resident DJ, and from there Layo and Matthew started working and recording together as Layo and Bushwacka!. You can catch a flavour of this here:

https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCdq3eAKEU3OaFxuqvUrGR1Q

It was in 2014 that Layo and Zoë turned to restaurants, and they didn’t put a foot wrong. The current portfolio of Studio Paskin includes The Palomar, The Barbary (two locations), Michelin-starred Evelyn’s Table and upstairs wine bar The Mulwray.

The Barbary Neal’s Yard is a special, intimate place. The 24 covers are all arranged around a large, extended-U-shaped counter, with the kitchen in the middle. The centrepiece of the kitchen is a hybrid charcoal/gas grill, and there’s also a tandoor-style oven.

The service is assured and the food is fantastic. It’s all about flavour, but the experience of eating here transcends merely food and drink. There’s energy and vibe, as well as the visual spectacle of the kitchen in action.

The wine list is short and well chosen, offering a lot of variety, with most of the wines available by the glass. We drank the Druida Dão Branco from Mira d’O and a fleshy, perfumed Jura red (Domaine des Carlines Poulsard).

title: Highlights: Klein Constantia Vin de Constance 2023 South Africa
summary: This is the latest vintage of one of the world’s great sweet wines. It’s the Vin de Constance from Klein
content:
This is the latest vintage of one of the world’s great sweet wines. It’s the Vin de Constance from Klein Constantia. This was a wine that was globally famous, then production stopped, and then it was rebirthed. It’s a brilliant story.

In the 18th and 19th centuries, Constantia was one of the world’s great sweet wines, but it disappeared with the phylloxera crisis in the late 19th century. I got to try an 1875 Constantia once, which was a remarkable treat:

Constantia Sweet Wine 1875
Aromatics of leather, spices, old furniture. Raisins and table groups with some honey, too. Lovely aromatics: sweetly smelling and quite pristine. Honeyed and smooth on the palate with real harmony, and notes of barley sugar, grapes, stewed raisins, bread pudding and smooth honeyed harmony. There’s lovely weight here. Such a treat to drink this. 96/100 (it seems silly to rate this)

But since 1986 Klein Constantia have been making a sweet Constantia which they have labelled Vin de Constance based on the original wines that were made here. Back then, the original Constantia property was around 900 hectares. Part of the original vineyard is now suburbs, and the remainder is split into four properties: Klein Constantia, Buitenverwachting, Groot Constantia and Constantia Uitsig.

When the Jooste family bought the property in 1980, they were approached by a Stellenbosch University viticulturist, the late Professor Chris Orffer, who encouraged them to try to recreate the historical sweet wine. Orffer assisted the Klein Constantia team in their quest to produce something resembling the original as closely as possible, and through the skilled winemaking of the late Ross Gower, this was achieved.

Botrytis wasn’t a feature of the original Constantia wines, because it wasn’t present in the Cape at that time. For the sake of historical accuracy, therefore, Vin de Constance is a late-harvest style made from Muscat de Frontignan (aka Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains), without botrytis. First plantings of these Muscat vines were in 1982, and the first release of the new era Constantia was the 1986 vintage, released in 1990.

10% of the Muscat is picked early, to make a base wine at 12.5–13 % alcohol with good acidity. Then they go and pick raisined grapes. The remaining grapes are left to accumulate sugar, and leaf removal exposes the fruit to the sun, which helps raisin some of the remaining grapes. Then, the big pick takes place in three passages through the vineyard. Altogether, around 10% of the crop will be raisins. If the raisins have lots of sugar left in the skins, then the base wine is used to flush the flavour out. The skins are pressed quite hard, because tannins are an important part of the style. It’s not possible to settle this dirty juice, so it goes to barrels for fermentation.

Klein Constantia Vin de Constance 2023 Constantia, South Africa
14% alcohol. This has wonderful youthful, grapey, floral aromatics with a hint of raisin and spice. Initially it seems forward, sweet and easy, but there’s some spicy complexity lying under the vivid ester and terpenic prettiness. The palate is complex and intense with some spicy structure, as well as table grape, apricot and peach fruitiness. It’s sweet and rich, but serious, with lots of potential to develop in bottle. Currently the intense sweetness and primary fruity aromas are dominating, but there’s a lot to come here. This is a wine with an amazing track record. At the UK price (£60 for the 2022 at the Wine Society), I think this is a bargain if you are going to cellar it for a decade. You are drinking history. 95/100

title: Highlights: Champagne Bruno Paillard Blanc de Blancs 2015
summary: Champagne Bruno Paillard Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut 2015 France12% alcohol. From the warm, dry vintage of 2015 this is
content:
Champagne Bruno Paillard Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut 2015 France
12% alcohol. From the warm, dry vintage of 2015 this is 100% Chardonnay from the first pressings of grapes from Grand and Premier Cru vineyards in the Côtes des Blancs. It’s aged 7 years on lees, disgorged in June 2023 with a 3 g/l dosage. This is really harmonious, with some rich yellow fruit (plum, peach) as well as a touch of apple, and then some brioche and honey on the nose. The palate has some of this richness, but also some fresh crystalline citrus fruit and good acidity, together with a hint of structure. The solar vintage makes its mark: this has concentration and mineral extract, as well as some ripeness of fruit. But there’s no undue bitterness or harshness: everything works together really well. A beautifully complex Champagne. 95/100

title: Restaurants: Heard Burger, Soho, with some lovely Lambrusco Sorbara
summary: Website: https://heardburger.co.uk/ I’ve been to a few good Burger places of late. There’s the brilliant Gasoline Grill in Copenhagen, and
content:
Website: https://heardburger.co.uk/

I’ve been to a few good Burger places of late. There’s the brilliant Gasoline Grill in Copenhagen, and then I had the amazing cheeseburger at Fat Rabbit in St Catherines (Niagara, Canada) twice in a week. And while we were filming in Burgundy there was a brilliant roadside burger place in Beaune (Yellow Bus Burger). But Heard, a relatively new opening near Carnaby Street in London, is as good as I’ve tasted.

There’s limited space upstairs, so most of the action happens in the basement. As a space, it lacks any real character, but the burgers here are amazing. Thrilling even. And what got me excited was that they have a Laumbrusco Sorbara on the menu for £40. It’s a good one, too, from Cleto Chiarli. But for some bizarre reason, on the short but good wine list, this was in with the red wines. It’s a pale pink fizz, and anyone ordering this as a red will be quite surprised.

This is as good as burgers get, and a strong recommend from me, especially if you like wine with your burger (most people were drinking their burgers with soft drinks when we visited mid-afternoon, something I find hard to understand.)

Cleto Chiarli Vecchia Modena Lambrusco di Sorbara Premium 2024 Italy
11.5% alcohol. Comes sealed with an afgrafe (a cork with a metal clip). Pale pink in colour. Tart, bright and lively with a bit of bite as well as sweet cherry and cranberry fruit. So juicy and delicious with a tangy sherbetty finish, juicy and alive. 91/100

title: Highlights: Morandé La Capilla El Cabernet de Ránguil 2021 Itata, Chile
summary: This is quite special. It’s from 130 year old ungrafted vines planted on degraded granite soils, with a red clay
content:
This is quite special. It’s from 130 year old ungrafted vines planted on degraded granite soils, with a red clay matrix and a high presence of quartz. Aged 16 months in used French oak. It’s not normal to find old vine Cabernet in Itata: most of the old vines here are País, Cinsault or Muscat.

Morandé La Capilla El Cabernet de Ránguil 2021 Itata, Chile
13% alcohol. This is supple and elegant with delicious, fresh, slightly gravelly blackcurrant fruit with a chalky, mineral texture and nice focus and precision. There’s a faint hint of that Chilean pastille-like fruit character. It’s a refined, understated, elegant expression of this grape variety showing beautiful poise and balance. Supple and massively drinkable, but with some seriousness, too. I much prefer this style to the very ripe, full-on Chilean Cabernet Sauvignon style. 94/100

title: Highlights: Cecilia Wines Cinsault 2025 Piekenierskloof, South Africa
summary: Cinsault was historically a hugely important red grape for South Africa. Often, red ‘varietals’ from fancy grapes like Cabernet Sauvignon
content:
Cinsault was historically a hugely important red grape for South Africa. Often, red ‘varietals’ from fancy grapes like Cabernet Sauvignon would have a good dollop of Cinsault in them. It’s a variety that copes with heat really well, making fresh, lighter-style red wines. There’s less of it now than there used to be, but still quite a bit, and its time has come.

Cecilia Wines Cinsault 2025 Piekenierskloof, South Africa
13% alcohol. Cerina van Niekerk was previously the winemaker at Piekenierskloof. Supple and sappy with lovely red cherry and strawberry fruit. There’s a real focus to this wine, with some silkiness and sweetness to the fruit, with a touch of redcurrant, but also showing a bit of tannic crunch and good acidity. This is a beautiful expression of Cinsault, so nuanced and floral. 93/100 (£22 Stone Vine and Sun)

title: Excursions off the Riesling trail: what else can the Finger Lakes do?
summary: This was a seminar at the FLXcursion event that I’ve just come back from. The goal was to explore the
content:
This was a seminar at the FLXcursion event that I’ve just come back from. The goal was to explore the things that the Finger Lakes can do other than Riesling, its star grape variety.

The panel consisted of three: Tara Q Thomas, Jamal Rayyis and Lisa Granik. They pointed out that in 1900 there were 20 000 acres of grapes in the Finger Lakes region and they sold 7 million bottles of sparkling wine each year. The grape industry was thriving, and it was huge. Then prohibition came along, and after the war tastes changed. California was doing well, and the European sensibility in wine spread, and people changed their tastes away from the hybrid fare of New York State’s wine regions.

Then along came Konstantin Frank. At age 63 he started his own winery and he changed the view on whether or not it was possible to grow vinifera here. The industry is still reinventing itself, and there are still a lot of hybrids here. But we are now seeing a return to interesting hybrid grapes to make wines with personality.

These are the wines we tasted.

Konstantin Frank Rkatsiteli 2025 Finger Lakes, New York
A relatively neutral grape variety with thick skins and tending to low acidity. Light and open with nice citrus and white peach fruit as well as some citrus. Fruity and appealing with good texture and weight. Easy and expressive. 90/100

Casreli Marani Amidasturi 2022 Kakheti, Georgia
This is a blend of Kakhuri Mtsvane, Muskaturi Rkatsiteli and Rkatsiteli. Four months in qvevri on skins, no stems. Amber colour. Nice structure here with some tannins under the appealing, aromatic peach and pear fruit with a touch of baked apple. Has nice weight and structure. Really appealing, finishing firmly tannic and dry, but with such lovely open aromatics. 91/100

Standing Stone Saperavi 2024 Finger Lakes, New York
Deeply coloured, this has beautiful fresh black cherry and blackberry fruit with some nice grippy tannins and some acidity. Juicy but with some richness on the mid-palate. Chalky, grainy structure with a spicy bite on the finish. 93/100

Mukado Saperavi 2024 Kartli, Georgia
This is pure and nicely balanced with some tannic bite. Lovely berry and black fruits with some chalkiness and a bit of spicy detail on the finish. Really appealing. 93/100

Fox Run Grüner Veltliner 2025 Finger Lakes, New York
Nice weight here with pear and citrus fruit showing good texture, finishing fresh. Juicy and layered with a slight saltiness and a hint of white pepper. This is textural and appealing with nice layers of flavour. 92/100

Fritsch Ried Steinberg Grüner Veltliner 2023 Wagram, Austria
This is bright and textured with nice weight to the pear and citrus fruit with some herbal hints, some yellow fruit, and then some pepper notes on the finish. Nice weight to this wine. 92/100

Heart & Hands Esoterra 2023 Finger Lakes, New York
56% Auxerrois, 23% Aligoté, 21% Petite Arvine. This is supple, easy and fruity with sweet pear and table grape notes, a touch of creaminess and balanced acidity. Fruity and easy to drink. 89/100

Ambrositch Gemischter Satz Ringelspiel 2021 Wien, Austria
12 varieties. Fresh, fine, transparent and easy. Mandarin, lemon and pear fruit with a touch of watermelon. Light and expressive with real drinkability. 90/100

Keuka Lake Vineyards Skin-Fermented Vignoles 2021 Finger Lakes, New York
Two weeks on skins. Full yellow/gold. Good acidity and some tannic structure as well as apple and pear fruit. Nice weight here: it’s floral but there’s some spicy weight, finishing slightly sour. 90/100

Fio Wines Mosel Glou Glou 2024 Mosel, Germany
Pinot Blanc and Müller Thurgau. A month on skins. Cloudy with some nice mineral reductive character as well as fresh pear and citrus fruit. Juicy and expressive with freshness and nice texture. So drinkable and delicious. 93/100

Usonia Good Spirit 2024 Finger Lakes, New York
65% Chancellor, 20% Baco Noir, 10% Traminette, 5% Aromella. Some spicy bacon character here as well as juicy cherry and berry fruits. Fresh and supple with some pepper on the finish. Juicy and expressive. 90/100

Vignoble de Rêveur Rien ne Bouge 2023 Vin de France
60% Pinot Noir, 30% Pinot Gris, 10% Pinot Blanc. This is from Alsace. Lighter in colour, this is supple and smashable with red cherry and strawberry fruit. Such elegance and freshness with beautiful fruit. Fresh with nice acidity but also showing some silky red fruit character. Very fine. 94/100

Ravines Agricolae Keuka Red 2022 Finger Lakes, New York
Noiret and Cabernet Franc. Concentrated, textural and pepper with nice spicy detail and a bit of grip. Really distinctive and quite delicious. Grainy structure. 90/100

Marco Sara Schioppettino Friuli Colli Orientali 2023 Italy
Bright, peppery and vivid with some strawberry and red cherry. Textural and expressive, this is lighter in style but also has some mid-palate warmth. Textural and expressive. 92/100

title: Weingut Frank, one of the stars of Austria’s Weinviertel region
summary: Website: https://www.weingutfrank.at/englishhome Frank is a family winery that’s been going since 1724. They’re currently farming 23 hectares of vines, and
content:
Website: https://www.weingutfrank.at/englishhome

Frank is a family winery that’s been going since 1724. They’re currently farming 23 hectares of vines, and they are based in the far north east of the region.

They specialize in Grüner Veltliner, but also like to work with Riesling, which is 10% of their vineyard. ‘Grüner Veltliner is our most important variety, but we love Riesling,’ says Katrin Frank, who I tasted with at the 2026 edition of Vievinum. These are impressive wines.

Weingut Frank Grüner Veltliner Kalk Loess 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
11.5% alcohol. Fresh, linear and bright with a core of citrus fruit. Mineral and expressive with nice intensity and a hint of pepper. Stylish. 91/100

Weingut Frank Grüner Veltliner 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
12.5% alcohol. Linear, bright and fresh with nice tension, and some peppery notes. Good citrus drive with a crystalline quality to the fruit. Pure. 92/100

Weingut Frank Grüner Veltliner Ried Johannisberger 2024 Weinviertel, Austria
Single vineyard with loess soils. Stainless steel ageing on full lees. Textural with notes of white pepper, showing good focus and intensity. Peppery and fruity. 93/100

Weingut Frank Grüner Veltliner Ried Hoher Weg 2024 Weinviertel, Austria
13.5% alcohol. This is a smaller part of Johannisberger. It’s a windy spot, and soils are loess. 600 litre barrels. Textured, fine and delicate with some depth. White pepper, pear and citrus. Rounded, but finishes slightly salty. 94/100

Weingut Frank Gruner Veltliner Baumgartleiten 2023 Weinviertel, Austria
Limestone soils, raised in 600 litre barrels. Fresh, linear and textured. Bright with nice energy, and some notes of pepper as well as crystalline lemons. Lovely focus and refinement. 94/100

Weingut Frank Riesling Herrnbaumgarten 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
A village-level wine. Linear, bright and fruity with lovely texture. So expressive with nice fresh fruit but also good texture. Really good. 93/100

Weingut Frank Pinot Blanc Herrnbaumgarten 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
Linear, bright and fruity. Textural and expressive with nice fresh fruit but also some nice texture. 92/100

Weingut Frank Zweigelt 2024 Weinviertel, Austria
Lovely floral aromatics here. Really pure and vivid, and so inviting. Nice fruit, with a pure, joyful personalitu and good balance. 93/100

Weingut Frank Ried Krainholz Pinot Noir 2023 Wienviertel, Austria
The soils are a mix of loess and limestone. Supple and elegant with fine red cherry fruit as well as some strawberry, with fine-grained structure. Such a lovely wine. 94/100

title: The wines of Weingut Kurz, Weinviertel, Austria
summary: Website: https://www.weingutkurz.at/ Eric Kurz (pictured above) is the third generation in this family winery, and makes 80% white wines and 20%
content:
Website: https://www.weingutkurz.at/

Eric Kurz (pictured above) is the third generation in this family winery, and makes 80% white wines and 20% red, working alongside his parents Paul and Erika. Grüner Veltliner is the main variety here. They are based in the centre of the region. I tasted with Eric at VieVinum in May 2026.

Kurz Welschriesling 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
13% alcohol. Aromatic, with a floral, fresh fruity drive. Fresh, stony and bright on the palate, showing nice weight to the dry fruit. Driven and expressive. 90/100

Kurz G’Mischta 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
12% alcohol. A blend of Grüner Veltliner and Riesling with a dribble of Sauvignon. Aromatic, fruity and expressive with nice juicy citrus and a bit of grapiness. Bright and slightly pithy. 90/100

Kurz Ried Edelsbergen Grüner Veltliner 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
13% alcohol. Fresh and juicy with nice brightness to the citrus fruit. Light and very fruity, showing good precision and nice weight. Understated. 91/100

Kurz Ried Hainthal Grüner Veltliner 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
Juicy, supple and bright with nice energy, and a slight peppery edge to the layered citrus fruit. Pure with good acidity and some freshness. 91/100

Kurz Ried Hochberg Sauvignon Blanc 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
12.5% alcohol. Delicately herby. Subtle, stony and bright, with nice purity. Has a lightness to it. 88/100

Kurz Ried Kirchbergen Gelber Muskateller 2025 Weinviertel, Austria
13% alcohol. Bright and fruity with nice grippy detail and some citrus drive. Juicy and lively with nice herbal hints. 89/100

Kurz Grüner Veltliner Ried Hungherm Reserve 2022 Weinviertel, Austria
14% alcohol. Aged in new French oak for 18 months. Full yellow in colour, this is bold and rich with notes of melon, pear and peach. Nicely intense with good weight. Low-ish acidity, with notes of honey and toast in the mix. Bold style. 91/100

Kurz Roesler Ried Edelsbergen Weinviertel, Austria
14% alcohol. There are just 400 hectares of Roesler (a red grape) in Austria, and this is a blend of 2023 and 2024 aged in American oak. Deeply coloured, this is bold, rich, spicy and dense with vivid spicy notes alongside the sweet black fruits. Satisfyingly broad. 90/100

title: Highlights: Soalheiro Nature Pur Terroir Alvarinho 2022 Portugal
summary: Really impressed by this fizz from Soalheiro in Vinho Verde. Soalheiro Nature Pur Terroir Alvarinho 2022 Vinho Espumante IG Minho,
content:
Really impressed by this fizz from Soalheiro in Vinho Verde.

Soalheiro Nature Pur Terroir Alvarinho 2022 Vinho Espumante IG Minho, Portugal
No added sulfites. Amazing freshness, structure and texture here. Pure, pithy citrus fruit with a touch of apple and some yeasty, spicy detail. This tastes nourishing and complete, with a nice savoury dimension to the fruit as well as some grapefruit pith. Such focus and interest, it’s one of the best sparkling wines I’ve tried from Portugal. There’s massive gastronomic possibility with this wine. 93/100

title: Champagne Perrier Jouët have implemented regenerative viticulture in half of their vineyard area, and they are already seeing differences in the base wines
summary: Jamie Goode travels to Champagne to visit the vineyards of Perrier-Jouët. Five years ago they began trialling regenerative approaches and
content:
Jamie Goode travels to Champagne to visit the vineyards of Perrier-Jouët. Five years ago they began trialling regenerative approaches and now 33 of their 65 hectares of vines are farmed this way, with a view to becoming 100% regenerative by 2030. Significantly, they are already finding that their base wines taste different.

It’s mid-April, and I’m standing in one of the vineyard plots of Champagne Perrier-Jouët, in the Grand Cru village of Ambonnay. The buds on the vine are just bursting: these vineyards have dodged the spring frost that has afflicted some parts of the region this year, but there’s something very different about this plot, and the ones around it. While the rest of the surrounding vineyard area is bare and devoid of life, these are bursting with life and energy. The multispecies cover crops, sown in autumn, have been flourishing, and in particular the yellow of the mustard flower and the white flowers of the broad beans really catch the eye.

It was back in 2021 that they began with their regenerative farming project. The first year, 4 hectares of their 65 hectares of vines were farmed this way, and because of the ongoing success of these trials, they now have 33 hectares of their 65 hectares of vines farmed regeneratively.

Importantly, the trials were established in such a way that they could measure everything: it’s a scientific trial. So the vineyards enrolled in the program are separated into repeated blocks of three different treatments.

The first is the control, the témoin. This is the way that they have farmed up till now, and it’s certainly not a bad way of working. They haven’t used herbicides since 2000, so undervine weed control is done manually, by ploughing. Then the row itself is left grassed. This is a higher standard of viticulture than most of the region practices: in the Champagne vineyard of 33 000 hectares there are more than 16 000 growers, so many have small plots and this is not their main career. Herbicides are still widely used, because they are cheap and convenient.

Then we have two regenerative treatments. The structure of the vineyards in Champagne, with a high planting density of 10 000 vines per hectare, means that it’s hard to implement a full suite of regenerative practices because of the lack of space and the impact on an already close-planted vineyard of any extra competition. So their main approach has been to use multi-species cover cropping, with two different mixes.

The first is a biomass cover crop. This consists of fava bean (Vicia faba), rye, clover and alfalfa, which is sowed in September. This is then flattened in late April using a roller crimper, or is incorporated into the soil using a light discing. The roller crimper breaks the stems and terminates the growth, which then lies on the soil and acts as a mulch. This protects the surface of the soil, lowering soil temperatures and reducing water loss. In the increasingly warm growing seasons the region is experiencing, this is a benefit.

The idea behind the biomass crop is that as it decays it will release organic material into the soil. It also acts to fix atmospheric nitrogen. The crop has worked well, and Perrier-Jouët are seeing positive trends. Jessica Jazeron-Schneider, their vineyard transformation manager, points out some of the results. Compared with the control, the soil of both this and the second regenerative treatment (see below) has better porosity and structure. The cover can give up to 80 units of nitrogen, and they are seeing more pruning weight (an indicator of vigour), improved yields and also better quality grapes. They have also seen higher worm counts.

One of the concerns with having growth in the vineyard during the frost risk period is that cold air stays for longer, and they have seen this as a potential issue. But Ambonnay is not particularly frost-prone.

The second cover crop is the flower treatment. Here they have turnip, borage, fennel and mustard, as well as some alfalfa. The goal of this crop is to nourish the soil, as with biomass, but also to increase biodiversity. This crop is sown once every three years (the ones we are looking at are in their third year), and then it is flattened twice a year, once before vine treatment starts and then just before harvest. In the first mix alfalfa dominated everything, but they’ve now worked out the right ratios to sow.

They have two different sowing machines, because the floral cover requires the seeds to be just on the surface while the biomass crop is sown much more deeply. They will check the forecast before sowing in September an sow when rain is due shortly afterwards to help with germination.

The plan is to collect data for another three years, but already they have been impressed enough with the results that the plans are to include all their vineyards in the program by 2030.

Both of the cover crop treatments have seen an increase in yield of around 20%, while quality has improved.

And the most remarkable aspect about these trials is that already there’s a difference in the wines. With chef du cave Séverine Frerson I tasted through base wines from 2025 on two separate occasions, and was surprised by how different wines from the three treatments tasted. ‘At the beginning I wanted to analyse what this gave to the wine, and what the profile of each modality is,’ says Frerson.

We began with Chardonnay. These wines are from a plot in Oiri that has been divided into three: control, biomass cover and flower cover. All harvested same date, 25 August. Pressed in a 4 ton Willmes press, just using cuvée (the best bit of the press). The wines are fermented and aged in 2000 litre tanks.

Control (témoin)
Fruity, bright and lively with keen acidity. Rich citrus fruit with lots of fruit and some fine spicy hints.

Flower Cover
Juicy, bright and linear with lovely precision. Keen acidity supports bright lemony fruit, showing nice taut fruit. Leaner than control.

Biomass cover
Juicy and linear with nice keen citrus fruit and good acidity. Focused and purposeful. Tightest and leanest.

I asked Frerson: what differences do they find with their regenerative plots? ‘In terms of yield, flower cover gives the best yield,’ she says. ‘For nitrogen, the levels are higher with flower and biomass than the control. They wines have more colour intensity with biomass cover.’ Frerson says that flower cover gives more complexity and finesse in the wine, and the biomass cover gives more freshness – ‘it’s a very straight wine’. The profile of the regenerative wines are in line with the style of Perrier Jouët. ‘Biomass is very straight and vertical with a lot of freshness. The floral cover is more delicate: the texture of this wine is different.’ They have seen these differences since 2021. The control is never the best wine.

Then we tried Pinot Noir from Aÿ.

Control
Focused, fruity, lemony, a touch of cherry and green apple. Juicy and vivid, slightly compressed.

Flower cover
Much more expressive with bright lemon and mandarin fruit, as well as some cherry. Very bright and with lovely layers of flavour.

Biomass cover
Juicy and vivid with bright redcurrant, cherry and lemon fruit. Supple and with lovely acid line. Very fine.

To me, the two regenerative wines are much finer and more interesting.

They started tasting since 2021, and the first conclusion was that biomass is very interesting for Pinot Noir, and flower cover for Chardonnay is promising, but Severine thinks it is too early to decide. ‘Regenerative viticulture is very interesting for the style of Perrier Jouët,’ she says. ‘There are more floral notes, more fruitiness and more freshness.’ They want all their vineyards to be farmed regeneratively by 2030.

The real importance of this work is that it is proof of principle, showing other growers that this approach works. The hope is that others will adopt these regenerative practices and that the bare soils of Champagne in spring will be a thing of the past. Along these lines, several of partner growers are now showing an interest in regenerative viticulture, and they have a special team to work with them. They have received 25 growers into their vineyard to look at their regenerative program. The idea is to have a special contract for those who are going to farm regeneratively. They want to push these people towards this way of farming with knowledge support and higher prices for the grapes. The goal is to convince people it is a good choice for the soil and also the wine.

On my first visit in December 2025 I saw another element to their regenerative approach. This was a vitiforestry project, which they began in winter 2023. In a two hectare plot they have avoided the normal Champagne monoculture, and as well as planting vines they have given over 10% of the plot to biodiversity. They enlisted the help of naturalists and drew up a list of different species that would be suitable, and have planted in three strata: herbs, shrubs and trees. Of the 1000 shrubs and trees they planted, they got a 90% survival rate. It’s too soon to see the benefits, but the landscape of Champagne could really use a biodiversity injection like this, and as well as providing ecosystem services that may result in less spraying, this could make the vineyards more resilient in the face of climate change.

A film of the regenerative viticulture plots in Ambonnay:

[Disclosure: I was paid for my work with Perrier-Jouët, which included hosting a masterclass at Wine Paris, and helping with a press trip to explain the project to journalists in April 2026. ]

title: Restaurants: celebrating 10 years of the Barbary, Neal’s Yard, with its countertop-only dining spanning southern Europe to north Africa
summary: Website: https://www.thebarbary.co.uk/neals-yard Great meal last night at The Barbary, Neal’s Yard (London). It’s one of two locations for this amazing
content:
Website: https://www.thebarbary.co.uk/neals-yard

Great meal last night at The Barbary, Neal’s Yard (London). It’s one of two locations for this amazing restaurant, with the younger but larger sibling in Notting Hill.

Last night was a celebration of 10 years of The Barbary, and owners Layo and Zoë Paskin were present. Their story is a cool one: for many years they ran nightclubs and DJd, before turning to hospitality.

Back in 1995 Layo and Richard West (aka Mr C. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mr._C ) started nightclub ‘The End’.  Matthew Benjamin was the resident DJ, and from there Layo and Matthew started working and recording together as Layo and Bushwacka!. You can catch a flavour of this here:

https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCdq3eAKEU3OaFxuqvUrGR1Q

It was in 2014 that Layo and Zoë turned to restaurants, and they didn’t put a foot wrong. The current portfolio of Studio Paskin includes The Palomar, The Barbary (two locations), Michelin-starred Evelyn’s Table and upstairs wine bar The Mulwray.

The Barbary Neal’s Yard is a special, intimate place. The 24 covers are all arranged around a large, extended-U-shaped counter, with the kitchen in the middle. The centrepiece of the kitchen is a hybrid charcoal/gas grill, and there’s also a tandoor-style oven.

The service is assured and the food is fantastic. It’s all about flavour, but the experience of eating here transcends merely food and drink. There’s energy and vibe, as well as the visual spectacle of the kitchen in action.

The wine list is short and well chosen, offering a lot of variety, with most of the wines available by the glass. We drank the Druida Dão Branco from Mira d’O and a fleshy, perfumed Jura red (Domaine des Carlines Poulsard).

title: Highlights: Klein Constantia Vin de Constance 2023 South Africa
summary: This is the latest vintage of one of the world’s great sweet wines. It’s the Vin de Constance from Klein
content:
This is the latest vintage of one of the world’s great sweet wines. It’s the Vin de Constance from Klein Constantia. This was a wine that was globally famous, then production stopped, and then it was rebirthed. It’s a brilliant story.

In the 18th and 19th centuries, Constantia was one of the world’s great sweet wines, but it disappeared with the phylloxera crisis in the late 19th century. I got to try an 1875 Constantia once, which was a remarkable treat:

Constantia Sweet Wine 1875
Aromatics of leather, spices, old furniture. Raisins and table groups with some honey, too. Lovely aromatics: sweetly smelling and quite pristine. Honeyed and smooth on the palate with real harmony, and notes of barley sugar, grapes, stewed raisins, bread pudding and smooth honeyed harmony. There’s lovely weight here. Such a treat to drink this. 96/100 (it seems silly to rate this)

But since 1986 Klein Constantia have been making a sweet Constantia which they have labelled Vin de Constance based on the original wines that were made here. Back then, the original Constantia property was around 900 hectares. Part of the original vineyard is now suburbs, and the remainder is split into four properties: Klein Constantia, Buitenverwachting, Groot Constantia and Constantia Uitsig.

When the Jooste family bought the property in 1980, they were approached by a Stellenbosch University viticulturist, the late Professor Chris Orffer, who encouraged them to try to recreate the historical sweet wine. Orffer assisted the Klein Constantia team in their quest to produce something resembling the original as closely as possible, and through the skilled winemaking of the late Ross Gower, this was achieved.

Botrytis wasn’t a feature of the original Constantia wines, because it wasn’t present in the Cape at that time. For the sake of historical accuracy, therefore, Vin de Constance is a late-harvest style made from Muscat de Frontignan (aka Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains), without botrytis. First plantings of these Muscat vines were in 1982, and the first release of the new era Constantia was the 1986 vintage, released in 1990.

10% of the Muscat is picked early, to make a base wine at 12.5–13 % alcohol with good acidity. Then they go and pick raisined grapes. The remaining grapes are left to accumulate sugar, and leaf removal exposes the fruit to the sun, which helps raisin some of the remaining grapes. Then, the big pick takes place in three passages through the vineyard. Altogether, around 10% of the crop will be raisins. If the raisins have lots of sugar left in the skins, then the base wine is used to flush the flavour out. The skins are pressed quite hard, because tannins are an important part of the style. It’s not possible to settle this dirty juice, so it goes to barrels for fermentation.

Klein Constantia Vin de Constance 2023 Constantia, South Africa
14% alcohol. This has wonderful youthful, grapey, floral aromatics with a hint of raisin and spice. Initially it seems forward, sweet and easy, but there’s some spicy complexity lying under the vivid ester and terpenic prettiness. The palate is complex and intense with some spicy structure, as well as table grape, apricot and peach fruitiness. It’s sweet and rich, but serious, with lots of potential to develop in bottle. Currently the intense sweetness and primary fruity aromas are dominating, but there’s a lot to come here. This is a wine with an amazing track record. At the UK price (£60 for the 2022 at the Wine Society), I think this is a bargain if you are going to cellar it for a decade. You are drinking history. 95/100

title: Highlights: Champagne Bruno Paillard Blanc de Blancs 2015
summary: Champagne Bruno Paillard Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut 2015 France12% alcohol. From the warm, dry vintage of 2015 this is
content:
Champagne Bruno Paillard Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut 2015 France
12% alcohol. From the warm, dry vintage of 2015 this is 100% Chardonnay from the first pressings of grapes from Grand and Premier Cru vineyards in the Côtes des Blancs. It’s aged 7 years on lees, disgorged in June 2023 with a 3 g/l dosage. This is really harmonious, with some rich yellow fruit (plum, peach) as well as a touch of apple, and then some brioche and honey on the nose. The palate has some of this richness, but also some fresh crystalline citrus fruit and good acidity, together with a hint of structure. The solar vintage makes its mark: this has concentration and mineral extract, as well as some ripeness of fruit. But there’s no undue bitterness or harshness: everything works together really well. A beautifully complex Champagne. 95/100

title: Restaurants: Heard Burger, Soho, with some lovely Lambrusco Sorbara
summary: Website: https://heardburger.co.uk/ I’ve been to a few good Burger places of late. There’s the brilliant Gasoline Grill in Copenhagen, and
content:
Website: https://heardburger.co.uk/

I’ve been to a few good Burger places of late. There’s the brilliant Gasoline Grill in Copenhagen, and then I had the amazing cheeseburger at Fat Rabbit in St Catherines (Niagara, Canada) twice in a week.
